title: Heatwave in north India reduces tourist visits to the Taj Mahal - The News Mill
full_text:
A severe heatwave across Northern India on April 29 led to a significant decline in the number of tourists visiting the Taj Mahal in Agra, Uttar Pradesh. The monument, typically crowded, experienced thinner crowds as visitors struggled with the intense heat.
Officials confirmed that the heatwave caused a reduction in tourist footfall at the Taj Mahal. Anjali Singh, a tourist, told ANI, “Children wanted to visit the Taj Mahal. It is very hot here… We have completed our tour within an hour… We had to face a lot of problems due to the heat…”
On April 26, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) reported maximum temperatures ranging from 40 to 46 degrees Celsius across most of the country, excluding the western Himalayan region, northeast Bihar, and northeast India, where temperatures remained below 36 degrees Celsius. The highest temperature recorded was 46.9 degrees Celsius at Akola.
The IMD forecasted heatwave to severe heatwave conditions in isolated pockets of Himachal Pradesh on April 26 and heatwave conditions on April 27. Heatwave conditions were also predicted in isolated pockets of Jammu and Kashmir on April 26 and 27, and over Punjab, Haryana, Chandigarh, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, and Gujarat during the same period. Similar conditions were expected in parts of Chhattisgarh until April 28.
The district administration in Prayagraj issued an advisory on April 26 addressing the rising temperatures. District Magistrate Manish Kumar Verma advised, “We have changed the school timings, and schools will be closed by 12 pm so that children do not have to stay in the sun unnecessarily. Whenever anyone goes outside at this time, they should cover their head. The body should remain hydrated, and one should drink adequate amounts of water regularly to avoid dehydration.” He also urged people to avoid outings during peak heat and said, “And if they do, they should take full precautions. We are installing water ATMs wherever needed.”
On April 26, the IMD issued a heatwave alert for parts of northwest and central India, warning that scorching conditions were likely to continue for three days. The agency advised avoiding prolonged exposure to heat, wearing lightweight, light-coloured, and loose cotton clothing, and covering the head with a cloth, hat, or umbrella when outdoors.

full_text:
- UP Storm & Rain Alert In 17 Districts; Heatwave To Push Temp Over 45°C
Mixed weather pattern witnessed in UP:Storm & rain forecasted for 17 districts; severe heatwave to push temperature above 45°C in coming days
- Copy link
The spell of storms and rain continues across Uttar Pradesh. On Saturday (May 9), a rain alert has been issued for 17 districts in Purvanchal, with winds expected to reach speeds of up to 50 kmph during this period.
In contrast, weather conditions remained clear since morning across western UP and several other regions, with bright sunshine observed during the day. However, cool breezes in many areas have helped provide relief from the prevailing heat.
Western disturbance weakens, temperatures set to rise again
The Meteorological Department has stated that the ongoing western disturbance is gradually weakening and is expected to end completely by today.
After its withdrawal, temperatures are likely to rise steadily over the next three days, with a possible increase of 5–6°C. In some areas, the mercury may once again cross the 45°C mark, leading to intense heat conditions.
However, officials have indicated that relief from heatwave conditions is likely to continue for the time being.
Rain, hail and strong winds disrupt life in parts of UP
In the past 24 hours, several districts of Uttar Pradesh, including Agra, Jalaun, Lalitpur and Mau, witnessed rain accompanied by strong winds. Hailstorm activity was also reported in Mau, while wedding preparations in Lalitpur were disrupted due to sudden weather changes.
Meanwhile, most other cities such as Lucknow, Kanpur, Noida and Ghaziabad experienced clear weather conditions. Varanasi recorded the highest temperature in the state at 39°C, making it the hottest location during the period.
4 flights cancelled in Lucknow
Bad weather conditions in Bihar, Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh has also affected air operations in Lucknow, leading to the cancellation of four flights to and from the city.
An IndiGo flight from Kolkata was diverted to Lucknow after it was unable to land at Patna airport due to poor visibility and adverse weather conditions. The aircraft reportedly circled in the air for some time before being safely diverted.
‘Ongoing spell of rain likely to pause’
Senior scientist Atul Kumar Singh at the Regional Meteorological Centre said, “The ongoing spell of rain in the state since April 30 is now likely to pause, which may lead to a rise in temperatures and a gradual intensification of summer conditions. However, relief from heatwave situations will continue for now”.
He further noted that continuous rainfall had already brought down maximum temperatures by around 9°C. While Banda had recorded a peak of 47.6°C on April 28, it dropped to around 39°C by May 8. He also indicated that a fresh weather system is expected to become active after two days, which may trigger another spell of rain.

Why fluctuations in weather?
The weather department reports continuous changes in the hilly regions of Uttarakhand, with snowfall in some areas, rainfall in others, and intermittent clear spells with sunshine. These fluctuations are expected to continue for the next 2–3 days.
Their impact is also being felt in Uttar Pradesh, where winds from the mountains are keeping temperatures moderate and supporting conditions favourable for rain.

full_text:
9 killed in Bihar as lightning, falling trees wreak havoc:Thunderstorms lash 6 states, including UP, Rajasthan's Barmer sizzles above 44°C
- Copy link
An alert for storms, rain, and lightning strikes has been issued for 17 districts of Uttar Pradesh on Saturday, and key cities Agra, Mau, and Jalaun in Uttar Pradesh received rain. And the authorities have issued an alert of a rainstorm in 13 districts of Madhya Pradesh.
States like Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Himachal Pradesh, and Rajasthan in North India experienced rain and hailstorms on Friday. Meanwhile, intense heat has started again in Central India.
Bihar's Patna and 7 other districts experienced heavy rain and storms on Friday, which caused more than 600 trees to fall in Patna.
Meanwhile, 9 people died due to falling trees and lightning in the state. Out of these, 5 deaths occurred in the capital Patna itself.
Hail also fell in Phulwarisharif and 4 people died due to lightning strikes in Bhojpur, Patna, Samastipur, and East Champaran.
Meanwhile, the heat has intensified in Rajasthan due to rising temperatures. Barmer was the hottest place in the country, where the mercury was recorded at 44.6 °C. Meanwhile, the temperature in Jaisalmer and Phalodi was 44 °C. Tonk in Rajasthan received rain on Friday afternoon.
On Friday, the daytime temperature in Madhya Pradesh rose. In Ratlam, the mercury reached 43.5 degrees Celsius. In Ahmedabad, Gujarat, the temperature was recorded at 43.1 °C, and in Washim, Maharashtra, it was 42.8 °C.
Hailstones fell in Shimla, covering the road with a 4-inch-thick layer
Shimla, Himachal Pradesh, experienced rain and hailstorms on Friday. This resulted in a layer more than four inches thick covering the road. JCB machines later had to be deployed to clear it. Anti-hail nets, installed to protect apple crops and orchards from hailstones, also broke.
